question_text: An ionized H-molecule consists of an electron and two protons. The protons are separated by a small distance of the order of angstrom. In the ground state, the electron would not move in circular orbits. the energy would be (2) 4 times that of an H-atom. the electrons, the orbit would go around the protons. the molecule will soon decay in a proton and an H-atom.
question_html:
An ionized H-molecule consists of an electron and two protons. The protons are separated by a small distance of the order of angstrom. In the ground state,

  1. the electron would not move in circular orbits.
  2. the energy would be (2)4 times that of an H-atom.
  3. the electrons, the orbit would go around the protons.
  4. the molecule will soon decay in a proton and an H-atom.
solution_text: (i, iii) In a hydrogen atom, electron revolves around a fixed proton-nucleus in a circular path. This can be explained by the Bohr model. But in the case of ionized H-molecule which consists of two protons in the nucleus and where protons are separated by a small distance of the order of angstrom, cannot be explained by the Bohr model. Hence, in this case, the ground state the electron would not move in circular orbits, the electrons orbit would go around the protons.
solution_html: (i, iii) In a hydrogen atom, electron revolves around a fixed proton-nucleus in a circular path. This can be explained by the Bohr model. But in the case of ionized H-molecule which consists of two protons in the nucleus and where protons are separated by a small distance of the order of angstrom, cannot be explained by the Bohr model. Hence, in this case, the ground state the electron would not move in circular orbits, the electrons orbit would go around the protons.

question_text: The Bohr model for the spectra of an H-atom will not be applicable to hydrogen in the molecular form. will not be applicable as it is for a He-atom. is valid only at room temperature. predicts continuous as well as discrete spectral lines.
question_html:
The Bohr model for the spectra of an H-atom

  1. will not be applicable to hydrogen in the molecular form.
  2. will not be applicable as it is for a He-atom.
  3. is valid only at room temperature.
  4. predicts continuous as well as discrete spectral lines.
solution_text: (i, ii) Bohr proposed a-model for hydrogen atom which is also applicable for some lighter atoms in which a single electron revolves around a stationary nucleus of positive charge Ze (called a hydrogen-like atom, e.g.: H, He + , Li +2 , Na +1, etc). It is not applicable to hydrogen in the molecular form and also, it will not be applicable as it is for a He-atom.
solution_html:
(i, ii)
Bohr proposed a-model for hydrogen atom which is also applicable for some lighter atoms in which a single electron revolves around a stationary nucleus of positive charge Ze (called a hydrogen-like atom, e.g.: H, He+, Li+2, Na+1, etc). solution_text: When an electron jumps from a higher energy level to the first energy levels, the spectrum of light is called the Balmer series. Spectrum lines become closer if electron jumps from higher energy level to ground level. Hence verifies answers (ii) and (iv).

question_text: Let E n = {tex}\frac{-1}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2}} \frac{m e^{4}}{n^{2} h^{2}}{/tex} be the energy of the nth level of H-atom. If all the H-atoms are in the ground state and radiation of frequency {tex}\frac {E_2 - E_1}h{/tex} falls on it, it will not be absorbed at all. some of the atoms will move to the first excited state. all atoms will be excited to the n = 2 state. no atoms will make a transition to the n = 3 state.
question_html:
Let En = {tex}\frac{-1}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2}} \frac{m e^{4}}{n^{2} h^{2}}{/tex} be the energy of the nth level of H-atom. If all the H-atoms are in the ground state and radiation of frequency {tex}\frac {E_2 - E_1}h{/tex} falls on it,

  1. it will not be absorbed at all.
  2. some of the atoms will move to the first excited state.
  3. all atoms will be excited to the n = 2 state.
  4. no atoms will make a transition to the n = 3 state.
solution_text: (ii, iv) Let E 2 and E 1 be the energy corresponding to n = 2 and n = 1 respectively. If radiation of energy {tex}\Delta{/tex} E = (E 2 - E 1 ) = h f incident on a sample where all the H-atoms are in the ground state, according to Bohr model some of the atoms will move to the first excited state. As this energy is not sufficient for the transition from n = 1 to n = 3, hence no atoms will make a transition to the n = 3 state.
solution_html: (ii, iv) Let E2 and E1 be the energy corresponding to n = 2 and n = 1 respectively. If radiation of energy {tex}\Delta{/tex}E = (E2 - E1) = hf incident on a sample where all the H-atoms are in the ground state, according to Bohr model some of the atoms will move to the first excited state. As this energy is not sufficient for the transition from n = 1 to n = 3, hence no atoms will make a transition to the n = 3 state.

question_text: The mass of an H-atom is less than the sum of the masses of a proton and electron. Why is this?
question_html: The mass of an H-atom is less than the sum of the masses of a proton and electron. Why is this?
solution_text: During the formation of an atom with nucleon and electrons, it needs the energy to bind the nucleons together in the nucleus. This energy comes from Einstein mass-energy relation E = {tex}\Delta{/tex} mC 2 Where {tex}\Delta{/tex} m = [Zm p + (A - Z) m n ] - M So the mass of an H atom is {tex}m_{p}+m_{p}-\frac{B \cdot E}{C^{2}}{/tex} This is clearly less than sum of the masses of a proton and an electron. Where B.E. = 13.6 eV
solution_html:
During the formation of an atom with nucleon and electrons, it needs the energy to bind the nucleons together in the nucleus. This energy comes from Einstein mass-energy relation
E = {tex}\Delta{/tex}mC2
Where {tex}\Delta{/tex}m = [Zmp + (A - Z) mn] - M
So the mass of an H atom is
{tex}m_{p}+m_{p}-\frac{B \cdot E}{C^{2}}{/tex}
This is clearly less than sum of the masses of a proton and an electron.
Where B.E. = 13.6 eV

question_text: Imagine removing one electron from He 4 and He 3 . Their energy levels, as worked out on the basis of Bohr model will be very close. Explain why?
question_html: Imagine removing one electron from He4 and He3. Their energy levels, as worked out on the basis of Bohr model will be very close. Explain why?
solution_text: After removing one electron from He 4 and He 3 , both the atoms will have only one electron and both will be a hydrogen-like atom. So, their energy levels, as worked out on the basis of Bohr model will be very close. Alternatively we can say that as both the residual nuclei are very heavy compared to the mass of electron removed, therefore, their energy levels, worked out on the basis of Bohr model, will be very close.

So, their energy levels, as worked out on the basis of Bohr model will be very close. question_text: When an electron falls from a higher energy to a lower energy level, the difference in the energies appears in the form of electromagnetic radiation. Why cannot it be emitted as other forms of energy?
question_html: When an electron falls from a higher energy  to a lower energy level, the difference in the energies appears in the form of electromagnetic radiation. Why cannot it be emitted as other forms of energy?
solution_text: As the electrons are charged particles, so when an electron falls from a higher energy level to a lower energy level, it gets accelerated. And we know that accelerated charged particle radiates energy in the form of electromagnetic radiation.

question_text: Would the Bohr formula for the H-atom remain unchanged, if proton had a charge (+ {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} )e and electron a charge (‒ {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} )e, where e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 ‒19 C. Give reasons for your answer.
question_html: Would the Bohr formula for the H-atom remain unchanged, if proton had a charge (+{tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex})e and electron a charge (‒{tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex})e, where e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10‒19 C. Give reasons for your answer.
solution_text: The Bohr formula involves only the product of the charges which remain constant for given values of charges. If proton had a charge (+ {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} )e and electron had a charge (‒ {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} )e, then the Bohr formula for the H-atom remain same.

question_text: Consider two different hydrogen atoms, the electron in each atom is in an excited state. Is it possible for the electrons to have different energies but the same orbital angular momentum according to the Bohr model?
question_html: Consider two different hydrogen atoms, the electron in each atom is in an excited state. Is it possible for the electrons to have different energies but the same orbital angular momentum according to the Bohr model?
solution_text: As per the Bohr model, {tex}E_n=-\frac{me^4}{8\varepsilon^2h^2n^2}{/tex} . Now if electrons have different energies, it means they have different values of n. So, their angular momentum, {tex}L_n = \frac{{nh}}{{2\pi }}{/tex} will also be different.

question_text: Positronium is just like an H-atom with the proton replaced by the positively charged anti-particle of the electron (called the positron which is as massive as the electron). What would be the ground state energy of positronium?
question_html: Positronium is just like an H-atom with the proton replaced by the positively charged anti-particle of the electron (called the positron which is as massive as the electron). What would be the ground state energy of positronium?
solution_text: As in the new H-atom (positronium) proton is replaced by positron of mass m = {tex}\frac {m_e }2{/tex} as under Mass of positronium = m = {tex}m_{e}^{-}+m_{e}^{+}{/tex} {tex}m_{e}^{+}=m_{e}^{-}=\frac{m_{e}}{2}{/tex} As E n = -13.6 and so the energy of the positron {tex}E_{n}=\frac{-m_{}^{} e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0} n^{2} h^{2}}=\frac{-\left(\frac{m_{e}}{2}\right) e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0} n^{2} h^{2}}=\frac{-13.6}{2} {/tex} So E n = {tex}-\frac{13.6}{2}\left(\because m_{}=\frac{m_e}{2}\right){/tex} E n = -6.8 eV
solution_html:
As in the new H-atom (positronium) proton is replaced by positron of mass m = {tex}\frac {m_e }2{/tex} as under
Mass of positronium = m = {tex}m_{e}^{-}+m_{e}^{+}{/tex}
{tex}m_{e}^{+}=m_{e}^{-}=\frac{m_{e}}{2}{/tex}
As En = -13.6 and so the energy of the positron
{tex}E_{n}=\frac{-m_{}^{} e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0} n^{2} h^{2}}=\frac{-\left(\frac{m_{e}}{2}\right) e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0} n^{2} h^{2}}=\frac{-13.6}{2} {/tex}
So En = {tex}-\frac{13.6}{2}\left(\because m_{}=\frac{m_e}{2}\right){/tex}
En = -6.8 eV

question_text: Show that the first few frequencies of light that is emitted when electrons fall to the n th level from levels higher than n are approximate harmonics (i.e. in the ratio 1 : 2: 3...) when n >>1.
question_html: Show that the first few frequencies of light that is emitted when electrons fall to the nth level from levels higher than n are approximate harmonics (i.e. in the ratio 1 : 2: 3...) when n >>1.
solution_text: When an electron falls from (n + p) th to nth energy level, the frequency of radiations in spectrum H-atom like atoms is given as {tex}v=C R Z^{2}\left[\frac{1}{\left(n+p\right)}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right]{/tex} Here n >> p n’ =1,2,3, …….. R= Rydberg’s constant {tex}v=C R Z^{2}\left[\frac{1}{n^{2}\left[1+\frac{p}{n^{2}}\right]^{2}}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}C R Z^{2}\left[\frac{1}{n^{2}}\left[1+\frac{p}{n}\right]^{-2}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right]{/tex} Neglecting the higher terms as n >> n’ {tex}=C R Z^{2}\left[\frac{1}{n^{2}}\left(1-\frac{2 p}{n}\right)-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right]=C R Z^{2}\left[\frac{1}{n^{2}}-\frac{2 p}{n^{3}}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-C R Z^{2} 2 p}{n^{3}}=\left(\frac{2 C R Z^{2}}{n^{3}}\right) p{/tex} So the first few frequencies of light that is emitted when electrons fall from (n+n’) to nth energy level are in the ratio of n’=1 : 2 : 3,….when n…1.
solution_html:
When an electron falls from (n + p)th to nth energy level, the frequency of radiations in spectrum H-atom like atoms is given as
{tex}v=C R Z^{2}\left[\frac{1}{\left(n+p\right)}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right]{/tex}
Here n >> p
n’ =1,2,3, ……..
R= Rydberg’s constant
{tex}v=C R Z^{2}\left[\frac{1}{n^{2}\left[1+\frac{p}{n^{2}}\right]^{2}}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}C R Z^{2}\left[\frac{1}{n^{2}}\left[1+\frac{p}{n}\right]^{-2}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right]{/tex}
Neglecting the higher terms as n >> n’
{tex}=C R Z^{2}\left[\frac{1}{n^{2}}\left(1-\frac{2 p}{n}\right)-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right]=C R Z^{2}\left[\frac{1}{n^{2}}-\frac{2 p}{n^{3}}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-C R Z^{2} 2 p}{n^{3}}=\left(\frac{2 C R Z^{2}}{n^{3}}\right) p{/tex}
So the first few frequencies of light that is emitted when electrons fall from (n+n’) to nth energy level are in the ratio of n’=1 : 2 : 3,….when n…1.

question_text: What is the minimum energy that must be given to an H-atom in ground state so that it can emit an {tex}H_\gamma{/tex} line in Balmer series? If the angular momentum of the system is conserved, what would be the angular momentum of such {tex}H_\gamma{/tex} photon?
question_html: What is the minimum energy that must be given to an H-atom in ground state so that it can emit an {tex}H_\gamma{/tex} line in Balmer series? If the angular momentum of the system is conserved, what would be the angular momentum of such {tex}H_\gamma{/tex} photon?
solution_text: {tex}H_\gamma{/tex} spectral line in Balmer series corresponds to transition of electron from n= 5 to n = 2. So, the electron in ground state n = 1 must first be put in state n = 5 for H-line. So, Energy of {tex}H_\gamma{/tex} , {tex}E=E_5-E_1=-\frac{13.6}{5^2}-\left(-\frac{13.6}{1^2}\right)eV=-0.54+13.6=13.06eV{/tex} If angular momentum is conserved, the angular momentum of H-atom = change in angular momentum of electron = {tex}L_{5}-L_{2}=\frac{5 \mathrm{h}}{2\pi}-\frac{2 \mathrm{h}}{2\pi}{/tex} = {tex}3 \times 1.06 \times 10^{-34}=3.18 \times 10^{-34}{/tex} kgm 2 /s
solution_html:
{tex}H_\gamma{/tex} spectral line in Balmer series corresponds to transition of electron from n= 5 to n = 2. So, the electron in ground state n = 1 must first be put in state n = 5  for H-line.
So, Energy of {tex}H_\gamma{/tex},  {tex}E=E_5-E_1=-\frac{13.6}{5^2}-\left(-\frac{13.6}{1^2}\right)eV=-0.54+13.6=13.06eV{/tex}
If angular momentum is conserved, the angular momentum of H-atom = change in angular momentum of electron
= {tex}L_{5}-L_{2}=\frac{5 \mathrm{h}}{2\pi}-\frac{2 \mathrm{h}}{2\pi}{/tex} = {tex}3 \times 1.06 \times 10^{-34}=3.18 \times 10^{-34}{/tex} kgm2/s

question_text: The first four spectral lines in the Lyman series of an H-atom are {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1218 {tex}\overset\circ A{/tex} , 1028 {tex}\overset\circ A{/tex} , 974.3 {tex}\overset\circ A{/tex} and 951.4 {tex}\overset\circ A{/tex} . If instead of Hydrogen, we consider Deuterium, calculate the shift in the wavelength of these lines.
question_html: The first four spectral lines in the Lyman series of an H-atom are {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1218 {tex}\overset\circ A{/tex}, 1028{tex}\overset\circ A{/tex}, 974.3 {tex}\overset\circ A{/tex} and 951.4{tex}\overset\circ A{/tex}. If instead of Hydrogen, we consider Deuterium, calculate the shift in the wavelength of these lines.
solution_text: The reduced mass of H atom (mass defect) {tex}\mu_{n}{/tex} then {tex}\frac{1}{\mu_{H}}=\frac{1}{m_{e}}+\frac{1}{M}{/tex} (M is mass of H atom) {tex}\frac{1}{\mu_{H}}=\frac{M+m_{e}}{M \cdot m_{e}}=\frac{M\left[1+\frac{m_{e}}{M}\right]}{M \cdot m_{e}}\left(\because M>>>m_{e}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \mu_{M}=m_{e}\left[1+\frac{m_{e}}{M}\right]^{-1}=m_{e}\left[1-\frac{m_{e}}{M}\right]{/tex} For Deuterium M = 2M The reduced mass of Deuterium- {tex}\mu_{D}{/tex} {tex}\mu_{D}=m_{e}\left[1-\frac{m_{e}}{M}\right]\left[1+\frac{m_{e}}{M}\right]{/tex} m is the reduced mass of the electron and proton in H atom. So h {tex}\nu{/tex} = En i - En f {tex}\nu{/tex} {tex}=\frac{m e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{2}}\left[\frac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}\right]=\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda} \propto \mu \Rightarrow \lambda \propto \frac{1}{\mu}{/tex} For hydrogen and deuterium = {tex}\frac{\lambda_{H}}{\lambda_{D}}{/tex} So {tex}\frac{\lambda_{H}}{\lambda_{D}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{H}}{\mu_{D}}=\frac{m_{e}\left[1-\frac{m_{e}}{M}\right]}{m_{e}\left[1-\frac{m_{e}}{M}\right]\left[1+\frac{m_{e}}{2 M}\right]}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{D}=\left[1+\frac{m_{e}}{2 M}\right]^{-1} \lambda_{h}=\left(1-\frac{m_{e}}{2M}\right) \lambda_{H}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{D}}=\lambda_{\mathrm{H}}{/tex} (0.99973) {tex}\lambda_{DI} = \overset \circ A{/tex} = 1214 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{D} 2}{/tex} = 0.9973 {tex}\times{/tex} 1028 = 1025 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{D} 3}{/tex} = 0.9973 {tex}\times{/tex} 974 = 971 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{D 4}{/tex} = 0.9973 {tex}\times{/tex} 954 = 951 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex}
solution_html:
The reduced mass of H atom (mass defect) {tex}\mu_{n}{/tex} then
{tex}\frac{1}{\mu_{H}}=\frac{1}{m_{e}}+\frac{1}{M}{/tex} (M is mass of H atom)
{tex}\frac{1}{\mu_{H}}=\frac{M+m_{e}}{M \cdot m_{e}}=\frac{M\left[1+\frac{m_{e}}{M}\right]}{M \cdot m_{e}}\left(\because M>>>m_{e}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mu_{M}=m_{e}\left[1+\frac{m_{e}}{M}\right]^{-1}=m_{e}\left[1-\frac{m_{e}}{M}\right]{/tex}
For Deuterium M = 2M
The reduced mass of Deuterium-{tex}\mu_{D}{/tex}
{tex}\mu_{D}=m_{e}\left[1-\frac{m_{e}}{M}\right]\left[1+\frac{m_{e}}{M}\right]{/tex}
m is the reduced mass of the electron and proton in H atom.
So h{tex}\nu{/tex} = Eni - Enf
 {tex}\nu{/tex} {tex}=\frac{m e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{2}}\left[\frac{1}{n_{i}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{f}^{2}}\right]=\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda} \propto \mu \Rightarrow \lambda \propto \frac{1}{\mu}{/tex}
For hydrogen and deuterium = {tex}\frac{\lambda_{H}}{\lambda_{D}}{/tex}
So {tex}\frac{\lambda_{H}}{\lambda_{D}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{H}}{\mu_{D}}=\frac{m_{e}\left[1-\frac{m_{e}}{M}\right]}{m_{e}\left[1-\frac{m_{e}}{M}\right]\left[1+\frac{m_{e}}{2 M}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_{D}=\left[1+\frac{m_{e}}{2 M}\right]^{-1} \lambda_{h}=\left(1-\frac{m_{e}}{2M}\right) \lambda_{H}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{D}}=\lambda_{\mathrm{H}}{/tex}(0.99973)
{tex}\lambda_{DI} = \overset \circ A{/tex} = 1214 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{D} 2}{/tex} = 0.9973 {tex}\times{/tex} 1028 = 1025 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{D} 3}{/tex} = 0.9973 {tex}\times{/tex} 974 = 971 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_{D 4}{/tex} = 0.9973 {tex}\times{/tex} 954 = 951{tex}\overset \circ A{/tex}

question_text: Deuterium was discovered in 1932 by Harold Urey by measuring the small change in wavelength for a particular transition in 1 H and 2 H. This is because the wavelength of transition depends to a certain extent on the nuclear mass. If the nuclear motion is taken into account then the electrons and nucleus revolve around their common center of mass. Such a system is equivalent to a single particle with a reduced mass {tex}\mu{/tex} , revolving around the nucleus at a distance equal to the electron-nucleus separation. Here {tex}\mu{/tex} = me M/(me +M) where M is the nuclear mass and me is the electronic mass. Estimate the percentage difference in wavelength for the 1st line of the Lyman series in 1 H and 2 H. (Mass of 1 H nucleus is 1.6725 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –27 kg, Mass of 2 H nucleus is 3.3374 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –27 kg, Mass of electron = 9.109 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –31 kg.)
question_html: Deuterium was discovered in 1932 by Harold Urey by measuring the small change in wavelength for a particular transition in 1H and 2H. This is because the wavelength of transition depends to a certain extent on the nuclear mass. If the nuclear motion is taken into account then the electrons and nucleus revolve around their common center of mass. Such a system is equivalent to a single particle with a reduced mass {tex}\mu{/tex}, revolving around the nucleus at a distance equal to the electron-nucleus separation. Here {tex}\mu{/tex} = me M/(me +M) where M is the nuclear mass and me is the electronic mass. Estimate the percentage difference in wavelength for the 1st line of the Lyman series in 1H and 2H. (Mass of 1H nucleus is 1.6725 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–27 kg, Mass of 2H nucleus is 3.3374 {tex}\times{/tex}10–27 kg, Mass of electron = 9.109 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–31 kg.)
solution_html:
Total energy of electron in nth stable orbit in H or like atom is given by:
En = {tex}\frac{\mu Z^{2} e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{2} n^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\mu{/tex} = reduced mass of electron, proton and neutron (mass defect)
EH = {tex}\frac{\mu_{H}(1)^{2} e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{2}}\left[\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{H} e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{2}}\left[\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{M} e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{2}}\left[\frac{3}{4}\right]{/tex}
E = h{tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda_{H}=\frac{h}{E_{H}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore h \nu_{H}=\frac{\mu_{H} e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{2}} \frac{3}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\nu_H{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{H} e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{3}} \cdot \frac{3}{4}{/tex}
The percentage difference in the wavelength is given by = {tex}\frac{\left(\lambda_{D}-\lambda_{H}\right)}{\lambda_{H}} \times 100{/tex}
Percentage change in wavelength is given by:
or change {tex}\Delta E=\left[\frac{\lambda_{D}}{\lambda_{H}}-1\right] \times 100{/tex} ({tex} \because \Delta{/tex}E = E1 - E2) ...(i)
h{tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{2}}\left[\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{3}}\left[\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}=\frac{\mu e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{3}}\left[\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{\mu e^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} c h^{3}}\left[\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right]{/tex}
as {tex}\mu{/tex}= mass defect, e, {tex}\varepsilon{/tex}, c, and h are constants for an atom.
{tex}\therefore \lambda \propto \frac{1}{h}{/tex}
So eqn. 1st can be written as the percentage change in the wavelength is given by = {tex}\left[\frac{\mu_{H}}{\mu_{D}}-1\right] \times 100{/tex}
{tex}\because \mu=\frac{m_{e} M}{\left(M+m_{e}\right)}{/tex} (Given)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Percentage change in wavelength = {tex}\left[\frac{\frac{m_{e} M_{H}}{\left(M_{H}+m_{e}\right)}}{\frac{m_{e}+M_{D}}{\left(M_{D}+m_{e}\right)}}-1\right] 100{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda_{H}} \times 100=\left[\frac{M_{H}}{M_{D}} \frac{\left(M_{D}+m_{e}\right)}{\left(M_{H}+m_{e}\right)}-1\right] \times 100{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\frac{M_{H}}{M_{D}} \frac{M_{D}\left(1+\frac{m_{e}}{M_{D}}\right)}{M_{H}\left(1+\frac{m_{e}}{M_{H}}\right)}-1\right] \times 100{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\left(1+\frac{m_{e}}{M_{D}}\right)\left(1+\frac{m_{e}}{M_{H}}\right)^{-1}-1\right] \times 100{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\left(1+\frac{m_{e}}{M_{D}}\right)\left(1-\frac{m_{e}}{M_{H}}\right)-1\right] \times 100{/tex}
Me << MD so neglecting the higher degree term
{tex}\frac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda_{H}} \times 100=\left[1-\frac{m_{e}}{M_{H}}+\frac{m_{e}}{M_{D}}-\frac{m_{e} m_{e}}{M_{D} \cdot M_{H}}-1\right] \times 100{/tex}
= {tex}m_{e}\left[\frac{1}{M_{D}}-\frac{1}{M_{H}}\right] \times 100{/tex}
= 9.1 {tex}\times 10^{-31}\left[\frac{1}{3.3374 \times 10^{-27}}-\frac{1}{1.6725 \times 10^{-27}}\right] \times 100{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31+2}}{10^{-27}}\left[\frac{1.6725-3.3374}{3.3374 \times 1.6725}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\Delta \lambda \times 100}{\lambda_{H}}=\frac{-9.1 \times 10^{-3.27} \times 0.6649}{3.3374 \times 1.6725}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-6.05059 \times 10^{-2}}{5.5180}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\Delta \lambda \times 100}{\lambda_{H}}=-1.084 \times 10^{-2} {/tex} Decrease in wavelength
(-) sign shows that {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{D}}<\lambda_{\mathrm{H}}{/tex}

The Coulombian force provides centripetal force to revolve around nucleus. {tex}\therefore \frac{m_{e} v^{2}}{r_{A}}=\frac{-K e^{2}}{r_{A}^{2}} {/tex} ...(i) Here K = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} (-) sign shows the force of attraction. By Bohr's postulate, angular momentum = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} mvr A = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi}{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi m r_{A}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{m n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m^{2} r_{2}^{2} r_{4}}=\frac{K e^{2}}{r_{2}^{2}}{/tex} [From i] r A = {tex}\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m K e^{2}}{/tex} ...(ii) For ground state n =1 r A = {tex}\frac{h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m K e^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} 6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{(2 \times 3.14)^{2} 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 9 \times 10^{9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 6.63 \times 10^{-63+38-31-9}}{9.19 \times 1.6 \times 1.6 \times 4 \times 3.14 \times 4 \times 3.14}{/tex} = r A = 0.53 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} P.E. = {tex}\frac{-K e^{2}}{r_{4}}=\frac{-9 \times 10^{9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{0.53 \times 10^{-10}} \mathrm{J} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{-9 \times 1.6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{9}}{0.53 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{J}{/tex} P.E. = {tex}\frac{14.4}{0.53}{/tex} -27.17 - 27.2 eV {tex}\mathrm{K} \cdot E=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{m \cdot n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m^{2} r^{2}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m v^{2}}{/tex} (n = 1 for ground state) = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 3.14 \times 9 \times 10^{-31} \times 0.53 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.53 \times 10^{-10}} J{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.62 \times 6.62 \times 10^{-68+31+10+10}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 3.14 \times 18 \times 0.53 \times 0.53 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} e V{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.62 \times 6.62 \times 10^{-68+51-19}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 3.14 \times 18 \times 0.53 \times 0.53}{/tex} K.E. = 0.1373 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 eV = 13.7 eV P.E. = -27.2 eV Now for spherical nucleus of radius, $R$, electron moves charge inside the nucleus R >> r b then electron moves inside the nucleus. Then (r b is radius of new Bohr's orbit of revolving electron) Charge = {tex}\frac{e \cdot\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi r_{b}^{3}\right)}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}}{/tex} {tex}e^{\prime}=q_{2}=\frac{e r_{b}^{3}}{R^{3}}{/tex} q 1 = e {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r_{b}}=\frac{K e e^{\prime}}{r_{b}^{2}}{/tex} (By Coulomb's law) mvr b = {tex}\frac{n h}{2 \pi} \Rightarrow v_{b}=\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v_{b}}{/tex} (By Bohr's postulate) {tex}\therefore \frac{m}{r_{j}} \frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m^{2} r_{j}^{2}}=\frac{k e e^{*}}{r_{j}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}r_{b}=\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m k e e^{\prime}}{/tex} Now for ground state of H, n = 1 and {tex}e^{\prime}=\frac{e r_{A}^{3}}{R^{3}}{/tex} , then {tex}\therefore r_{b}=\frac{h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m K \cdot e \cdot e \cdot \frac{r_{b}^{3}}{R^{3}}}=\left(\frac{h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m K e^{2}}\right) \times \frac{R^{3}}{r_{3}^{3}}=r_{A} \times \frac{R^{3}}{r_{3}^{3}}{/tex} [ {tex}\because r_{A}=\frac{h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m K e^{2}}{/tex} =0.53 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} [calculated in part (i)] {tex}r_{b}=r_{A}\left(\frac{R}{r_{j}}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}r_{3}^{b}=r_{A} R^{3}=0.53 \overset \circ{A} \times(10 \overset \circ A)^{3}{/tex} {tex}r_{b}^{4}=0.53 \times 1000(\overset \circ{A})^{4}\left(\because r_{A}=0.53 \overset \circ A\right){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{b}}=\left[530 \mathrm{A}^{4}\right]^{1 / 4} \overset \circ A^{<\mathrm{R}} \overset \circ A{/tex} K.E. = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{m}{2} \cdot \frac{h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m^{2} r_{b}^{2}}=\frac{h^{2}}{8 \pi^{2} m r_{b}^{2}}\left[\because v=\frac{n^{2} h^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} m^{2} r_{b}^{2}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.62 \times 6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 10^{-34}}{8 \times 3.14 \times 3.14 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 4.8 \times 4.8 \times 10^{-20} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} e V{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.62 \times 6.62 \times 10^{-,68+31+20+19}}{8 \times 3.14 \times 3.14 \times 9.1 \times 4.8 \times 4.8 \times 1.6} e V{/tex} = {tex}\frac{43.8244}{26460.2} 10^{-68-70}{/tex} - 0.001656 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 eV K.M = 0.167 eV P.E. = {tex}=\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{\left(r_{3}^{2}-3 R^{2}\right)}{R^{3}}\left(P . E=\frac{K q_{1} q_{2}}{r}\right){/tex} P.E. = {tex}=\left[\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{\delta} \gamma_{A}}\right] \frac{r_{A}\left(r_{2}^{2}-3 R^{2}\right)}{R^{3}}{/tex} [multiyplying by {tex}\frac{r_{A}}{r_{A}}{/tex} ] From part (i) P.E. = {tex}\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r_{A}}{/tex} = 27.2 e V {tex}\therefore P . E .=27.2\left[\frac{0.53(\sqrt{530}-300)}{1000}\right]\left[\because r_{b}=(530)^{1 / 4} \overset \circ {A} \ {\text {and }} R=10 \overset \circ {A}\right.{/tex} = {tex}\frac{27.2 e V \times 0.53(23.02-300) \overset \circ A^{3}}{1000 \overset \circ {A}^{3}}=27.2 \times \frac{0.53(-276.9)}{1000} e V{/tex} P.E. = {tex}\frac{3992.9}{1000}{/tex} = -3.99 eV K.E. = 0.167 eV

question_text: In the Auger process an atom makes a transition to a lower state without emitting a photon. The excess energy is transferred to an outer electron which may be ejected by the atom. (This is called an Auger electron). Assuming the nucleus to be massive, calculate the kinetic energy of an n = 4 Auger electron emitted by Chromium by absorbing the energy from n = 2 to n = 1 transition.
question_html: In the Auger process an atom makes a transition to a lower state without emitting a photon. The excess energy is transferred to an outer electron which may be ejected by the atom. (This is called an Auger electron). Assuming the nucleus to be massive, calculate the kinetic energy of an n = 4 Auger electron emitted by Chromium by absorbing the energy from n = 2 to n = 1 transition.
solution_text: As the nucleus is massive, recoil momentum of the atom may be neglected and the entire energy of the transition may be considered transferred to the Auger electron. As there is a single valence electron in Cr, the energy states may be thought of as given by the Bohr model. The energy E n of the nth state E n = +Z 2 R {tex}\left[\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right]=Z^{2} R\left(\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{4}\right){/tex} (for n 1 = 1, n 2 = 2) Z = 24, R = Rydberg constant, {tex}\therefore E_{n}=\frac{3}{4} Z^{2} R{/tex} The energy required to eject an electron from n = 4 state is E 4 = Z 2 R {tex}\frac{1}{4^{2}}=\frac{1}{16} Z^{2} R{/tex} Energy given to electron is converted into K.E. of ejected electron. Hence, the K.E. of Auger (ejected) electron = E n - E 4 K.E. = Z 2 R {tex}\frac{3}{4}-\frac{1}{16} Z^{2} R=\frac{11}{16} Z^{2} R=\frac{11}{16} \times {/tex} 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 13.6 eV K.E. = 11 {tex}\times{/tex} 36 {tex}\times{/tex} 13.6 = 5385.6 eV
solution_html:
As the nucleus is massive, recoil momentum of the atom may be neglected and the entire energy of the transition may be considered transferred to the Auger electron. As there is a single valence electron in Cr, the energy states may be thought of as given by the Bohr model. The energy En of the nth state
En = +Z2R {tex}\left[\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right]=Z^{2} R\left(\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{4}\right){/tex}(for n1 = 1, n2 = 2)
Z = 24, R = Rydberg constant, 
{tex}\therefore E_{n}=\frac{3}{4} Z^{2} R{/tex}
The energy required to eject an electron from n = 4 state is
E4 = Z2R {tex}\frac{1}{4^{2}}=\frac{1}{16} Z^{2} R{/tex}
Energy given to electron is converted into K.E. of ejected electron.
Hence, the K.E. of Auger (ejected) electron = En - E4
K.E. = Z2R {tex}\frac{3}{4}-\frac{1}{16} Z^{2} R=\frac{11}{16} Z^{2} R=\frac{11}{16} \times {/tex} 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 13.6 eV
K.E. = 11 {tex}\times{/tex} 36 {tex}\times{/tex} 13.6 = 5385.6 eV

question_text: The inverse square law in electrostatics is {tex}|\vec{F}|=\frac{e^{2}}{\left(4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\right) \cdot r^{2}}{/tex} for the force between an electron and a proton. The {tex}\left(\frac{1}{r}\right){/tex} dependence of {tex}|\vec F|{/tex} can be understood in quantum theory as being due to the fact that the particle of light (photon) is massless. If photons had a mass m p , force would be modified to {tex}|\vec{F}|=\frac{e^{2}}{\left(4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\right) r^{2}}\left[\frac{1}{r^{2}}+\frac{\lambda}{r}\right]{/tex} .exp (- {tex}\lambda{/tex} r) where {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac {m_p c }{h}{/tex} and h = {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex} . Estimate the change in the ground state energy of an H-atom if m p were 10 –6 times the mass of an electron.
question_html: The inverse square law in electrostatics is {tex}|\vec{F}|=\frac{e^{2}}{\left(4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\right) \cdot r^{2}}{/tex} for the force between an electron and a proton. The {tex}\left(\frac{1}{r}\right){/tex} dependence of {tex}|\vec F|{/tex} can be understood in quantum theory as being due to the fact that the particle of light (photon) is massless. If photons had a mass mp, force would be modified to {tex}|\vec{F}|=\frac{e^{2}}{\left(4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\right) r^{2}}\left[\frac{1}{r^{2}}+\frac{\lambda}{r}\right]{/tex}.exp (-{tex}\lambda{/tex}r) where {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac {m_p c }{h}{/tex} and h = {tex}\frac{h}{2 \pi}{/tex}. Estimate the change in the ground state energy of an H-atom if mp were 10–6 times the mass of an electron.
solution_text: Here, we suppose mass of photon is given by, m p = 10 -6 electronic mass i.e., m p = 10 -6 (0.5) MeV = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -13 J = 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J. Now, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{h}{m_{p} c}=\frac{h c}{m_{p} c^{2}}=\frac{10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{0.8 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m, which is much larger than the Bohr radius. As {tex}|\vec{F}|=\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \in_{0}}\left[\frac{1}{r^{2}}+\frac{\lambda}{r}\right] \exp .(-\lambda r) {/tex} ...(i) where {tex}\lambda^{-1}=\frac{h}{m_{p} c}=4 \times 10^{-7} m{/tex} >> r B {tex}\therefore \lambda{/tex} < < {tex}\frac{1}{r_{B}} \text { or } \lambda_{B}{/tex} < < 1 Now, U(r) = {tex}\frac{-e^{2}}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \frac{\exp (-\lambda r)}{r}{/tex} ...(ii) As mvr = {tex}h\over 2\pi{/tex} =h or v= {tex}\frac{h}{m r}{/tex} . As exp(- {tex}\lambda{/tex} r) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1, therefore, form (i) {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=F=\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{1}{r^{2}}+\frac{\lambda}{r}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{m}{r}\left(\frac{h}{m r}\right)^{2}=\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \in_{0}}\left[\frac{1}{r^{2}}+\frac{\lambda}{r}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{h^{2}}{m}=\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \in_{0}} r+\lambda r^{2}{/tex} ...(iii) If {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0, r B , then {tex}\frac{h^{2}}{m}=\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} r_{B}{/tex} As {tex}\lambda{/tex} -1 > > r B , put r = r B + {tex}\delta{/tex} Form (iii), r B = (r B + {tex}\delta{/tex} ) + {tex}\lambda{/tex} (r B + {tex}\delta{/tex} ) 2 = r B + {tex}\delta{/tex} + {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\left(r_{B}^{2}+\delta^{2} 2 \delta r_{B}\right){/tex} by Neglecting {tex}\delta{/tex} 2 , we get 0 = {tex}\lambda r_{B}^{2}+\delta\left(1+2 \lambda r_{B}^{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}-\lambda r_{B}^{2}\left(A s \lambda r_{B}<<1\right){/tex} form (ii), V(r) = {tex}\frac{-e^{2}}{4 \pi \in_{0}} \frac{\exp .\left(-\lambda \delta-\lambda r_{B}\right)}{r_{B}+\delta}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-e^{2}}{4 \pi \in_{0}} \frac{1}{r_{B}}\left[\left(1-\frac{\delta}{r_{B}}\right)\left(1-\lambda r_{B}\right)\right]{/tex} = -27.2 eV hence, V(r) remains unchanged. K.E. = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{1}{2} m\left(\frac{h}{m r}\right)^{2}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m\left(r_{B}+\delta\right)^{2}}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m r_{B}^{2}}\left(1-\frac{2 \delta}{r_{B}}\right){/tex} = (13.6eV)[1 + 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} r B ] Thus, the Total energy is given by = {tex}\frac{-e^{2}}{4 \pi \in_{0} r_{B}}+\frac{h^{2}}{2 m r_{B}^{2}}\left(1+2 \lambda r_{B}\right){/tex} = -27.2 + 13.6(1 + 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} r B ) eV. Change in ground state energy of hydrogen atom is given by = 13.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} B eV = 27.2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} r B eV
solution_html:
Here, we suppose mass of photon is given by, mp = 10-6 electronic mass
i.e., mp = 10-6(0.5) MeV = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13J = 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19J.
Now, {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{h}{m_{p} c}=\frac{h c}{m_{p} c^{2}}=\frac{10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{0.8 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m, which is much larger than the Bohr radius.
As {tex}|\vec{F}|=\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \in_{0}}\left[\frac{1}{r^{2}}+\frac{\lambda}{r}\right] \exp .(-\lambda r) {/tex} ...(i) where {tex}\lambda^{-1}=\frac{h}{m_{p} c}=4 \times 10^{-7} m{/tex} >> rB
{tex}\therefore \lambda{/tex} < < {tex}\frac{1}{r_{B}} \text { or } \lambda_{B}{/tex} < < 1
Now, U(r) = {tex}\frac{-e^{2}}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \frac{\exp (-\lambda r)}{r}{/tex} ...(ii)
As mvr = {tex}h\over 2\pi{/tex} =h or v= {tex}\frac{h}{m r}{/tex}. As exp(-{tex}\lambda{/tex}r) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1, therefore, form (i) 
{tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=F=\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{1}{r^{2}}+\frac{\lambda}{r}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{m}{r}\left(\frac{h}{m r}\right)^{2}=\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \in_{0}}\left[\frac{1}{r^{2}}+\frac{\lambda}{r}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{h^{2}}{m}=\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \in_{0}} r+\lambda r^{2}{/tex} ...(iii)
If {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0, rB, then {tex}\frac{h^{2}}{m}=\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} r_{B}{/tex}
As {tex}\lambda{/tex}-1 > > rB, put r = rB + {tex}\delta{/tex}
Form (iii), rB = (rB + {tex}\delta{/tex}) + {tex}\lambda{/tex}(rB + {tex}\delta{/tex})2 = rB + {tex}\delta{/tex} + {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\left(r_{B}^{2}+\delta^{2} 2 \delta r_{B}\right){/tex}
by Neglecting {tex}\delta{/tex}2, we get 0 = {tex}\lambda r_{B}^{2}+\delta\left(1+2 \lambda r_{B}^{2}\right){/tex}
= {tex}-\lambda r_{B}^{2}\left(A s \lambda r_{B}<<1\right){/tex}
form (ii),
V(r) = {tex}\frac{-e^{2}}{4 \pi \in_{0}} \frac{\exp .\left(-\lambda \delta-\lambda r_{B}\right)}{r_{B}+\delta}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-e^{2}}{4 \pi \in_{0}} \frac{1}{r_{B}}\left[\left(1-\frac{\delta}{r_{B}}\right)\left(1-\lambda r_{B}\right)\right]{/tex} = -27.2 eV
hence, V(r) remains unchanged.
K.E. = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{1}{2} m\left(\frac{h}{m r}\right)^{2}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m\left(r_{B}+\delta\right)^{2}}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m r_{B}^{2}}\left(1-\frac{2 \delta}{r_{B}}\right){/tex} = (13.6eV)[1 + 2{tex}\lambda{/tex}rB]
Thus, the Total energy is given by = {tex}\frac{-e^{2}}{4 \pi \in_{0} r_{B}}+\frac{h^{2}}{2 m r_{B}^{2}}\left(1+2 \lambda r_{B}\right){/tex} = -27.2 + 13.6(1 + 2{tex}\lambda{/tex}rB) eV.
Change in ground state energy of hydrogen atom is given by = 13.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2{tex}\lambda{/tex}BeV = 27.2{tex}\lambda{/tex}rBeV

question_text: The Bohr model for the H-atom relies on the Coulomb’s law of electrostatics. Coulomb’s law has not directly been verified for very short distances of the order of angstroms. Supposing Coulomb’s law between two opposite charge + q 1 , –q 2 is modified to {tex}|\vec{F}|=\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{\left(4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\right)} \frac{1}{r^{2}}, \quad r \geq R_{0}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{1}{R_{0}^{2}}\left(\frac{R_{0}}{r}\right)^{\epsilon}, r \leq R_{0}{/tex} Calculate in such a case, the ground state energy of an H-atom, if {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0.1, R 0 = 1 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex}
question_html:
The Bohr model for the H-atom relies on the Coulomb’s law of electrostatics. Coulomb’s law has not directly been verified for very short distances of the order of angstroms. Supposing Coulomb’s law between two opposite charge + q1, –q2 is modified to
{tex}|\vec{F}|=\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{\left(4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\right)} \frac{1}{r^{2}}, \quad r \geq R_{0}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{1}{R_{0}^{2}}\left(\frac{R_{0}}{r}\right)^{\epsilon}, r \leq R_{0}{/tex}
Calculate in such a case, the ground state energy of an H-atom, if {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0.1, R0 = 1{tex}\overset \circ A{/tex}
solution_text: Let us consider the case when r {tex}\leq{/tex} R 0 = 1 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} Let {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 2 + {tex}\delta{/tex} electrostatic force is given by F = {tex}\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{R_{0}^{\delta}}{r^{2+\delta}}=\frac{x R_{0}^{\delta}}{r^{2+\delta}}{/tex} where, {tex}\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} = x = {tex}\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{2} \times 9 \times 10^{9}{/tex} = 2.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -29 Nm 2 The electrostatic force of attraction between the positively charged nucleus and negatively charged electrons provides the necessary centripetal force. {tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=\frac{x R_{0}^{\delta}}{r^{2+\delta}} {/tex} or {tex}v^{2}=\frac{x R_{9}^{\delta}}{m r^{1+\delta}} {/tex} ...(i) mvr = nh {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{n h}{m v}=\frac{n h}{m}\left[\frac{m}{x R_{0}^{\delta}}\right]^{1 / 2} r^{(1+\delta) / 2}{/tex} [Applying Bohr's second postulates] Solving this for r, we get r n = {tex}\left[\frac{n^{2} \hbar^{2}}{m x R_{0}^{\delta}}\right]^{\frac{1}{1-\delta}}{/tex} where, r n is the radius of n th orbit of electron. For n = 1 and substituting the values of constant, we get r 1 = {tex}\left[\frac{h^{2}}{m x R_{0}^{\delta}}\right]^{\frac{1}{1-\delta}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad r_{1} =\left[\frac{1.05^{2} \times 10^{-68}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 2.3 \times 10^{-28} \times 10^{419}}\right]^{\frac{1}{29}} {/tex} = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 = 0.08 nm (< 0.1 nm) This is the radius of orbit of the electron in the ground state of the hydrogen atom. Again using Bohr's second postulate, the speed of electron is given by ; v n = {tex}\frac{n h}{m r_{n}}=n h\left(\frac{m \alpha R_{0}^{\delta}}{n^{2} \hbar^{2}}\right)^{\frac{1}{1-\delta}}{/tex} For n = 1, the speed of electron in ground state is given by v 1 = {tex}\frac{h}{mr_{1}}=1.44 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} The kinetic energy of electron in ground state is given by:- KE = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v_{1}^{2}=9.43 \times {/tex} 10 -19 J = 5.9 eV Potential energy of electron in ground state till $R_{0}$ U = {tex}\int_{0}^{R_{0}} F d r=\int_{0}^{R_{0}} \frac{x}{r^{2}} d r=-\frac{x}{R_{0}}{/tex} Potential energy from R 0 to r, U = {tex}\int_\limits{R_{0}}^{r} F d r=\int_\limits{R_{0}}^{r} \frac{x R_{0}^{\delta}}{r^{2+\delta}} d r^{\prime}{/tex} U = {tex}+x R_{0}^{\delta} \int_{R_{0}}^{r} \frac{d r}{r^{2+\delta}}=+\frac{x R_{0}^{\delta}}{-1-\delta}\left[\frac{\mathbf{1}}{r^{1+\delta}}\right]_{R_{0}}^{r}{/tex} U = {tex}-x\left[\frac{R_{0}^{\delta}}{r^{1+\delta}}-\frac{1}{R_{0}}+\frac{1+\delta}{R_{0}}\right] {/tex} U = {tex}-x\left[\frac{R_{0}^{-19}}{r^{-0.9}}-\frac{1.9}{R_{0}}\right] {/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.3}{0.9} \times 10^{-18}{/tex} [(0.8) 0.9 - 1.9] J = -17.3 eV Hence total energy of electron in ground state is given by= (-17.3 + 5.9) = -11.4 eV
solution_html:
Let us consider the case when r {tex}\leq{/tex} R0 = 1{tex}\overset \circ A{/tex}
Let {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 2 + {tex}\delta{/tex}
electrostatic force is given by F = {tex}\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{R_{0}^{\delta}}{r^{2+\delta}}=\frac{x R_{0}^{\delta}}{r^{2+\delta}}{/tex}
where, {tex}\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} = x = {tex}\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{2} \times 9 \times 10^{9}{/tex}
= 2.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-29 Nm2
The electrostatic force of attraction between the positively charged nucleus and negatively charged electrons provides the necessary centripetal force.
{tex}\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=\frac{x R_{0}^{\delta}}{r^{2+\delta}} {/tex} or {tex}v^{2}=\frac{x R_{9}^{\delta}}{m r^{1+\delta}} {/tex} ...(i)
mvr = nh {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = {tex}\frac{n h}{m v}=\frac{n h}{m}\left[\frac{m}{x R_{0}^{\delta}}\right]^{1 / 2} r^{(1+\delta) / 2}{/tex} [Applying Bohr's second postulates]
Solving this for r, we get rn = {tex}\left[\frac{n^{2} \hbar^{2}}{m x R_{0}^{\delta}}\right]^{\frac{1}{1-\delta}}{/tex}
where, rn is the radius of n th orbit of electron. For n = 1 and substituting the values of constant, we get
r1 = {tex}\left[\frac{h^{2}}{m x R_{0}^{\delta}}\right]^{\frac{1}{1-\delta}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r_{1} =\left[\frac{1.05^{2} \times 10^{-68}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 2.3 \times 10^{-28} \times 10^{419}}\right]^{\frac{1}{29}} {/tex}
= 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 = 0.08 nm (< 0.1 nm)
This is the radius of orbit of the electron in the ground state of the hydrogen atom.
Again using Bohr's second postulate, the speed of electron is given by ;
vn = {tex}\frac{n h}{m r_{n}}=n h\left(\frac{m \alpha R_{0}^{\delta}}{n^{2} \hbar^{2}}\right)^{\frac{1}{1-\delta}}{/tex}
For n = 1, the speed of electron in ground state is given by v1 = {tex}\frac{h}{mr_{1}}=1.44 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
The kinetic energy of electron in ground state is given by:-
KE = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v_{1}^{2}=9.43 \times {/tex} 10-19J = 5.9 eV
Potential energy of electron in ground state till $R_{0}$
